item.title: ‘Unconstitutional exercise of power’: National court revokes Ombudsman Commission’s directive to stop Starlink operating in PNG
item.description: THE National Court has quashed a directive by the Ombudsman Commission (OC) to revoke Starlink’s operating licence in the country. The court upheld National Information and Communications Technology Authority’s (Nicta) judicial review this afternoon in Waigani.
item.content:encoded:
THE National Court has quashed a directive by the Ombudsman Commission (OC) to revoke Starlink’s operating licence in the country.

The court upheld National Information and Communications Technology Authority’s (Nicta) judicial review this afternoon in Waigani.

National Court judge Oagile Dingake, who delivered the judgment on behalf of the presiding judge Susan Purdon-Sully, said OC’s directive to Nicta to revoke Starlink Internet Services (PNG) Ltd’s licence was “ultra vires and consequently null and void”.

“The court finds the direction an arbitrary and unconstitutional exercise of power issued without proper regard to the constitutional limits on the exercise of section 27(4) power, rendering it an excess of jurisdiction, liable to be quashed,” judge Purdon-Sully ruled.

The Ombudsman Commission on Feb 27, 2024, directed Nicta not to grant an operating network licence to Starlink.

Nicta sought judicial review which took almost two years for a decision to be made.

Nicta chief executive officer Lume Polume outside court said “the decision by the court appreciates the importance of connectivity.

“It relives the geographical constraints we face in PNG.

“This is an important milestone and with that court decision, it gives Nicta the opportunity to continue to discharge our mandate,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Disgraceful neglect of PNG’s birds of paradise
item.description: WHAT we today reveal in our exclusive coverage of the fate of our birds of paradise is as shocking as it is sad. It seems from our cursory observation and from the conversations we have had or attempted to have with agencies mandated to the conservation of the environment, there seems little care or concern shown the unique aviary assets of this country.
item.content:encoded:
WHAT we today reveal in our exclusive coverage of the fate of our birds of paradise is as shocking as it is sad.

It seems from our cursory observation and from the conversations we have had or attempted to have with agencies mandated to the conservation of the environment, there seems little care or concern shown the unique aviary assets of this country.

Of 44 known species domicile in Australia, Indonesia and the island of New Guinea, 38 are found in New Guinea which includes Indonesian West Papua.

Of these, 12 are only to be found in PNG.

It is shocking in the extreme because a class of birds so exquisitely beautiful, so unique as to define and stand this country apart from every other country in the world, may now be endangered.

It is sad because their protection should be the first thought that comes to mind when this country speaks of the conservation of its unique flora and fauna – the protection of its botanical and biological wealth.

What could be more unique, so priceless as to transcend the rarest gems?

For these are not dead, cold stones; they are living, breathing and flying birds, glittering gems of every colour and description flitting through the tropical jungles of this country like ghostly protectors of the forest itself.

It is sad again because their endangerment should be so glaringly obvious – what with the monetary and cultural value placed on their feathers; what with a growing population that demands their feathers to support a tourism industry spawned around cultural festivities that in all cases need birds of paradise feathers; and what with the advent of guns, with which the birds can be hunted to extinction in the space of a year.

Government organisations this newspaper has talked to do not have any idea of the current status or welfare of the birds in their natural habitat.

There are no major aviaries or parks dedicated to their preservation except for tourist-oriented sites such as the Variata Park outside Port Moresby.

The Government is clueless to the fate of the birds, one of which – Paradisaea Raggianna – adorns the flag of the country; is at once the emblem of state, and is the image that descends out of the sky onto foreign capitals on the aircrafts of Air Niugini, the national airline.

“ It is sad because their protection should be the first thought that comes to mind when this country speaks of the conservation of its unique flora and fauna – the protection of its botanical and biological wealth.”

The national rugby league team is named “Kumul” (or bird and popularly inferred as the bird of paradise).

All of the country’s extractive resources companies are banded under the Kumul Consolidated Holdings Ltd.

The national broadcaster is styled the “Krai bilong Kumul” – the voice of the bird of paradise.

The first ever engagement by the PNG Defence Force in an overseas conflict was in 1981 when the Kumul Force put down a rebellion on Espirito Santo island in Vanuatu, enabling that Melanesian neighbour to gain independence.

Again, a bird-of-paradise force delivered that successful mission.

The bird is minted on the currency in coins and emblazoned on the K50 note flying across the image of Parliament.

On the other side of the same note is the image of the first Prime Minister Sir Michael Thomas Somare.

Both the bird of paradise and Chief Somare are synonymous with Papua New Guinea.

If the man is remembered, it stands to reason that the bird must be too.

Every bird of paradise is a thing of worth in every traditional culture in PNG.

In every traditional singsing, every dance step, every flapping arm and every nodding head adorned with feathers from a wide variety of birds of paradise, is the perfect mimicking of the prideful amorous steps, the wide-flung magnificent feathers in all their resplendent glory, and the shrill cries that seek to surpass the competitor for the attention of the female.

The bird of paradise is the heart-beat of this country.

To ignore its fate is to ignore the fate of this country.

The fourth National Goal and Directive Principle provides for the protection and preservation of PNG’s natural environment.

The bird of paradise represents all of that; it represents all that is best in Papua New Guinea.

It behooves Parliament to pass a Birds of Paradise Protection Act at its next sitting and to draw up the supporting regulations, including setting up reserves for birds of paradise in every province and in every location where they are to be found in the natural habitat.

Hunting of them and commercial trading in feathers or of the birds themselves must be banned, with very heavy penalties for transgressions.

item.title: ENB authorities confirm 94 cyclone hit site
item.description: AUTHORITIES in East New Britain (ENB) have confirmed that 94 sites across the province were severely impacted by Tropical Cyclone Maila which swept through the region last week. An estimated 65,400 people across the four districts of Rabaul, Kokopo, Gazelle and Pomio were affected by heavy rain, flooding, landslides and strong winds.
item.content:encoded:
AUTHORITIES in East New Britain (ENB) have confirmed that 94 sites across the province were severely impacted by Tropical Cyclone Maila which swept through the region last week.

An estimated 65,400 people across the four districts of Rabaul, Kokopo, Gazelle and Pomio were affected by heavy rain, flooding, landslides and strong winds.

As part of the immediate activation of disaster relief, restoration of accessibility was prioritised to reach affected communities.

A physical count identified 94 locations where residents were directly impacted.

Assessments were carried out through the normal government mechanisms, from local level governments (LLGs) to district and provincial authorities.

One death was reported in Kokopo, another in Rabaul, and 10 in the Gazelle’s Inland-Baining local level government area.

ENB deputy administrator (corporate services) Nicholas Larme said the provincial team was working tirelessly to reach affected families.

“We’ve done our best to make sure that we console those families who severely affected and provide them with relief supplies,” he said.

Larmes added that relief efforts would continue for weeks to come.

“We will maintain this support for the next month or so, ensuring food and building materials reach the most severely affected areas.’

The Division of Lands and Physical Planning was the first within the provincial administration to donate relief supplies, a gesture publicly acknowledged by the coordination team.

Many families remain displaced, with food and building materials being provided to help them restart their lives.

Relief assistance will continue to flow into communities as authorities work to stabilise the situation and support recovery.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Constabulary commissions four senior officers
item.description: THE Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary has officially commissioned four senior officers into higher ranks, marking the first promotion list of 2026 and signalling a renewed era of leadership within the force. The ceremony was officiated by Police Commissioner David Manning, supported by Deputy Commissioners Dr Philip Mitna (administration) and Samson Kua (regional operations).
item.content:encoded:
THE Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary has officially commissioned four senior officers into higher ranks, marking the first promotion list of 2026 and signalling a renewed era of leadership within the force.

The ceremony was officiated by Police Commissioner David Manning, supported by Deputy Commissioners Dr Philip Mitna (administration) and Samson Kua (regional operations).

Commissioner Manning in congratulating the four officers said: “Leadership is not about the title you wear, but the difference you make in the lives of our people.

“Today, you are not just promoted – you are entrusted with the hopes of communities who look to you for safety and justice.

“Acceptance of promotion is one thing, but belief in your leadership is another.

“The Constabulary needs leaders who will not only command but inspire.”

Manning also challenged the four promoted officers to continue the good work that they had started in their respective commands.

“You have proven yourselves in acting roles. Now, as confirmed leaders, you must rise above challenges, strengthen discipline, and restore public confidence in our force,” Manning said.

The Police Commissioner confirmed that while this was the first promotion list of the year, additional promotions were expected across the ranks – from non-commissioned officers to chief superintendents – reinforcing the Constabulary’s commitment to renewal, discipline, and strengthened leadership.

The Commander rank, once dormant, has been revived to strengthen leadership role across operational areas of the Police Force.

Positioned above chief superintendent and a rank below the assistant commissioner, the commander rank provides clearer authority and accountability.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Veupunama crush Hetura as PomNA heats up
item.description: VEUPUNAMA delivered a commanding performance on Saturday, dismantling Hetura 57-19 in the first round campaign of the Port Moresby Netball Association (PomNA) campaign. Coach Margaret Opina’s side set the tone early, tightening their defence in the first quarter while shooters Kala Ali and Violet Tom converted with precision.
item.content:encoded:
VEUPUNAMA delivered a commanding performance on Saturday, dismantling Hetura 57-19 in the first round campaign of the Port Moresby Netball Association (PomNA) campaign.

Coach Margaret Opina’s side set the tone early, tightening their defence in the first quarter while shooters Kala Ali and Violet Tom converted with precision.

Hetura’s defenders were left scrambling as their attackers struggled to penetrate Veupunama’s defensive wall. The first quarter ended with Veupunama firmly ahead, 20-2.

Despite Hetura’s efforts to adjust, the second quarter brought little relief.

Veupunama’s speed and long passes proved too much, with Hetura managing only four shots to trail 34-6 at halftime.

At the break, Veupunama focused on fine-tuning combinations with new players stepping in, while Hetura were urged to stick to basics and build confidence under Christabella Amona’s guidance.

The third quarter saw Veupunama extend their lead to 45-12, with both teams rotating players to give everyone court time. The final whistle confirmed Veupunama’s dominance at 57-19.

Hetura will need to strengthen their combinations in both attack and defence, while Veupunama look to maintain their winning streak and prepare for tougher challenges ahead.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Lockyer’s positive on Chiefs
item.description: WIDELY regarded as one of greatest players in NRL history, former Queensland great Darren Lockyer sees positive in the PNG Chiefs project and the pathway systems, believing that the franchise can thrive without Australian players after five years in the National Rugby League (NRL) competition. Lockyer, who is known for his exceptional versatility as both a fullback and five-eighth, said the PNG Ch
item.content:encoded:
WIDELY regarded as one of greatest players in NRL history, former Queensland great Darren Lockyer sees positive in the PNG Chiefs project and the pathway systems, believing that the franchise can thrive without Australian players after five years in the National Rugby League (NRL) competition.

Lockyer, who is known for his exceptional versatility as both a fullback and five-eighth, said the PNG Chiefs project will inspire local youth and contribute to the country’s development. “I anticipate it to be competitive and successful both on and off the field.”

Lockyer added: “Initially, some Australian players might be needed, but the long-term goal is to rely on local talent through a robust pathway system which I believe PNG is starting to build.

“The pathways and coaching systems in PNG is already brilliant. And I believe they (PNG Chiefs) will not need to rely on Australians after five years.”

Lockyer played his entire professional career with the Brisbane Broncos, making his first-grade debut at just 18  in 1995. A dominant force in Australian and Queensland rugby league, Lockyer captained both the Queensland State of Origin team and the Kangaroos.

He said in Port Moresby yesterday that the PNG Chiefs project would create investment opportunities for business and investors, inspiring young children coming through the pathway systems and create a goal for PNG children to pursue the sport, especially in their pursuit to play for the national team (Kumuls) and the NRL franchise.

Commenting on the PNG Chiefs board, he described it as “astute and capable”, making the club a source of pride for the country.

“I am confident that the Chiefs will be competitive early and successful both on and off the field,” he said. “I am on the Brisbane Broncos board so I will not recommend anyone but the Chiefs will definitely need NRL players in their debut season.  The pathway systems here are developing well from what I am seeing so I am optimistic of the future and I would like to see more local talents in the side after five years.”

Lockyer, who is also the co-owner of the London Broncos in England’s Betfred Championship competition, said his Papua New Guinea players would be given options to choose to join the PNG Chiefs.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Super Sunday in nation’s capital
item.description: It will be a Super Sunday for rugby league fanatics in the National Capital District and Central as the Gas Resource Central Dabaris clash with the Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers in Round 3 of the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup at the Sir John Guise Stadium. The double-header continues at 4pm when the Kroton Hela Wigmen face off against the NPL Enga Mioks at the same venue.
item.content:encoded:
It will be a Super Sunday for rugby league fanatics in the National Capital District and Central as the Gas Resource Central Dabaris clash with the Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers in Round 3 of the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup at the Sir John Guise Stadium.

The double-header continues at 4pm when the Kroton Hela Wigmen face off against the NPL Enga Mioks at the same venue.

Adding to the excitement, local musician Taita Maraga will entertain the crowd throughout the day, while fans can also enjoy fixtures from the Under-17 academy boys and girls and the National Youth Competition Under-19 boys, featuring Dabaris against Vipers. In the U17 division, Central Academy will meet NCD Academy.

Both Dabaris and Vipers are locked neck-and-neck on the competition ladder after two rounds, making Sunday’s 2pm clash crucial for leadership.

Victory alone may not be enough, as margin matters with the Hela Wigmen close behind.

The Kroton-sponsored Wigmen are tipped for a comfortable outing against the Mioks in the main game.

All other Sunday matches will be held at Goroka’s National Sports Institute, Eastern Highlands, where Mendi Muruks play Waghi Tumbe, Lae Snax Tigers lock horns with Kandes, and Goroka Lahanis host Gulf Isou in the afternoon’s main match.

Meanwhile, Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League chief executive officer Stanley Hondina said in a statement on Wednesday that Round 3 is centred around mental health, discipline, and character, emphasising that strength is also about resilience, composure and leadership.

“Men in League (round) reminds players that true strength is shown in how they respond to pressure and how they carry themselves every day,” Hondina said, adding that respect, self-control and teamwork improve performance and also contribute to stronger communities.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Hunters aim to retain Kokoda Cup against Bears
item.description: THE SP PNG Hunters are determined to defend the Kokoda Cup as they prepare for a crucial Round 7 clash against the Burleigh Bears in the Hostplus Cup this weekend in Gold Coast, Australia. Coach Paul Aiton (pictured)  noted the significance of the Kokoda trophy, which the Hunters have only won twice in 11 years.
item.content:encoded:
THE SP PNG Hunters are determined to defend the Kokoda Cup as they prepare for a crucial Round 7 clash against the Burleigh Bears in the Hostplus Cup this weekend in Gold Coast, Australia.

Coach Paul Aiton (pictured)  noted the significance of the Kokoda trophy, which the Hunters have only won twice in 11 years.

“We won it last year and brought it here. I just reminded the boys to look at it and realise how much this means to both teams,” Aiton said.

He acknowledged the atmosphere in Burleigh, calling it “a really big event” and anticipating a tough contest.

The Hunters enter the match buoyed by a strong win over the Ipswich Jets last weekend.

Aiton confirmed the squad has recovered well despite the physical toll of that game.

“I think the fitness levels are good. They were just a little bit sore after that weekend’s game. It was a pretty long, tough game right to the last minute, so we just had to look after them a little bit this week,” he explained.

Minor lineup changes include Elijah Rotinga being rested to recover from a knee issue, while Koso Bandi will start in the front row and Clent Lama moves to the bench.

Defence remains the Hunters’ top priority. “We speak a lot about it, we train for it every week. If we can improve our defence, it’ll really help us in every other part,” Aiton said.

He also praised rising star Bruce Bawase, who scored a hat-trick last week.

“He’s only coming up to his seventh game now but he’s learning a lot and I’ve seen him grow very quickly in the game,” Aiton noted.

Player representative Josh Mire reinforced the team’s focus on sticking to their game plan.

“We will just stick to our structure, simple game plans the coach wants us to execute,” Mire said.

“We’ve been inconsistent after big games in the past, so we worked on that and we’ll maintain the momentum we built last week.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Scourge of unemployment hangs like a shroud over PNG
item.description: GRADUATION fever is spreading throughout the country as students from tertiary institutions dress in their best attire to receive their certificates, diplomas and degrees after years of hard work. Sadly, the future of the country’s next generation of human resource remains bleak as a serious plight continues to affect national development unemployment.
item.content:encoded:
GRADUATION fever is spreading throughout the country as students from tertiary institutions dress in their best attire to receive their certificates, diplomas and degrees after years of hard work.

Sadly, the future of the country’s next generation of human resource remains bleak as a serious plight continues to affect national development unemployment.

Unemployment is a forever scourge and afflicts nations at their most vulnerable state of development like a persistent illness that refuses to go away.

Just trying to earn a living by securing a paid job can seem like a never-ending nightmare.

A 2024 report from the National Research Institute, authored by Nellie Kati and Dr Philip Kavan, it highlighted the severe impact of unemployment on PNG’s socio-economic progress.

Titled Unemployment poses a significant threat to the development of Papua New Guinea , the report examines the risks associated with mass unemployment and proposes strategies to address the issue.

The report identifies several ways in which unemployment affects PNG’s development as it:

In order to mitigate these challenges, the report suggests several measures such as:

This is the view of the country’s own institution mandated to carry out independent research and analysis on development issues affecting PNG.

And from eyes from abroad looking in, the statistics about unemployment do not get any better.

The International Labour Organisation (ILO) — a specialised agency dealing with work and workplace issues, and related rights and standards — highlighted youth unemployment as a prevalent challenge affecting much of PNG’s labour market.

 In its 2024 country report, the ILO rated youth unemployment in PNG at about three times higher than the general population.

The ILO stated that the unemployment rate was 2.7 per cent and rising to 3.8 per cent for the youth in the country.

It added that youth unemployment is about three times higher than the general population, largely due to the low absorption of school leavers into the formal employment sector.

But this is a story many are all too familiar with.

For those living in Port Moresby, it has almost become a norm to see see long lines and scores of people gathered outside a company’s premises whenever walk-in interviews are announced.

This was evident outside the gates of a supermarket chain earlier this year when some 3,000 job seekers braved the heat just to secure a job.

Among them would have been university graduates and diploma holders, all with eager hearts hoping to get called in to be given an opportunity for a driver, cashier, shop assistant or supervisor positions.

The scene was very familiar to what had happened outside The Stanley Hotel and Suites on Feb 4, 2023.

The hotel had advertised vacancies with a target to interview about 1,000 applicants.

But no one expected the huge turnout of people who attended and were packed like a can of sardines outside the hotel.

Even during the PNG Youth Resource Week that was held last January when it would have been easy to spot crowds of people waiting outside the premises of the Sir John Guise Stadium.

They had waited patiently for hours for a chance to interact with key government agencies, businesses and other organisations to secure future career and educational opportunities.

These events and much more only go to show the plague of unemployment that has infected the lives of many Papua New Guineans.

However, the Government has been optimistic.

Labour and Employment Minister Kessy Sawang had announced recently that the Government has set an ambitious goal of creating one million jobs for the people by 2027.

She said that this was captured in the Medium-Term Development Plan Four, as part of the Government’s overall strategy to improve the country’s economic growth and gross domestic product.

But with the deadline for this goal expected in the next eight months, one has to ask if it is really possible to achieve.

And as the Government does its best to increase job opportunities for the people, other underlying issues such as corruption, poor infrastructure, and a limited access to basic health and education services must also be addressed as well.

Only then can there be a vibrant and well-educated workforce to drive socio-economic development of the country.

item.title: Recycled leaders erode democracy
item.description: DEMOCRACY relies on trust, accountability, and leaders acting in the public interest. Yet, many nations continue to allow politicians with corrupt track records – those implicated in embezzlement, misconduct, or serious ethical breaches – to re-enter office.
item.content:encoded:
DEMOCRACY relies on trust, accountability, and leaders acting in the public interest.

Yet, many nations continue to allow politicians with corrupt track records – those implicated in embezzlement, misconduct, or serious ethical breaches – to re-enter office.

This recycling of corrupt leaders erodes public trust and normalises corruption within governance.

It’s time to end this cycle and treat public service as a privilege, not a path to personal gain.

Political corruption undermines national resources meant for education, healthcare, and infrastructure.

The cycle of recycling corrupt leaders happens when:

Transparency International PNG and various civic groups argue that allowing corrupt politicians to return to office harms national development.

Voters must hold leaders accountable, examining their track record beyond party loyalty.

The message is clear: if you couldn’t fix it then, why should we believe you now?

It’s time to demand political integrity and disqualify those who breach public trust from ever holding office again.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Defending humanity and freedom of speech amid growing impunity
item.description: As the “Gaza Playbook” seemingly extends to Lebanon, including attacks on critical civilian infrastructure, humanitarian personnel and journalists, we present this summary of a December 2025 diascussion between panellists Amra Lee, Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan, Dr Annabel Dulhunty, Dr Ali Shaar, Dr Richard Brennan and Mudasser Siddiqui. In December last year, academics and practitioners came together at t
item.content:encoded:
As the “Gaza Playbook” seemingly extends to Lebanon, including attacks on critical civilian infrastructure, humanitarian personnel and journalists, we present this summary of a December 2025 diascussion between panellists Amra Lee, Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan, Dr Annabel Dulhunty, Dr Ali Shaar, Dr Richard Brennan and Mudasser Siddiqui.

In December last year, academics and practitioners came together at the 2025 Australasian Aid and International Development Conference to discuss human rights and humanitarian issues in Gaza.

While a ceasefire had technically been negotiated in October last year, over 500 violations had been documented as of November, alongside intensifying settler violence and annexation in the West Bank.

Opening the panel, Dr Annabel Dulhunty from the Australian National University (ANU) highlighted the problem of “a ceasefire that is not a ceasefire” and the challenge of universities shying away from discussions on Gaza.

Dr Ali Nashat Shaar, from An Najah University in the West Bank, underlined that an 18-year-old in Gaza today had already survived six wars.

Dr Cadhla O’Sullivan (ANU) described how all children’s rights had been violated, their existence reduced to mere survival.

Mudasser Siddiqui from Plan International said that, despite all this, after the ceasefire announcement, children in Gaza were sharing hope for the future.

Six months later, that ceasefire has failed to meet its own protection and humanitarian objectives, according to a coalition of non-government organisations.

Dr Rick Brennan, former WHO Regional Emergency Director for the World Health Organization (WHO) in the Eastern Mediterranean Region, and Amra Lee, practitioner and PhD researcher (ANU), unpacked the humanitarian consequences of Gaza and beyond – from over 70,000 deaths and 170,000 injuries, to record aid-worker deaths, hundreds of attacks on healthcare, soaring humanitarian needs and dramatic reductions in global funding.

They outlined the major operational constraints on the United Nations (UN)-led aid-delivery system, including the prevention of United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East (UNRWA) from bringing personnel and aid into Gaza.

Moreover, the establishment of the Gaza Humanitarian Foundation (GHF) during an unfolding famine demonstrated the risks of replacing principled aid delivery with inexperienced, politicised contractors during a complex and volatile humanitarian operation – risks that saw 1,397 Palestinians killed and another 4,000 injured trying to reach GHF food distribution sites.

At the session’s close, the panellists and interlocutors in the audience shared a collective sigh of sadness and relief – that it was possible to have a critical and respectful dialogue on Gaza.

That senior experts felt this tension in speaking about human suffering in Gaza, based on UN reports and data, requires critical reflection.

As Australian academic Na’ama Carlin has highlighted, there is a Gaza-shaped hole in curricula that has had far-reaching implications for Palestinians since Oct 7 2023.

Many educators have avoided discussing Gaza due to fear and lack of institutional support.

This fear has been exacerbated by pressure on institutions to adopt the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism – a definition not intended to be legally binding. Universities Australia adopted an IHRA-aligned definition in February last year.

The IHRA definition risks being interpreted and applied in ways that would prevent legitimate criticism of Israel’s conduct with respect to human rights and international law, exacerbating the chilling effect on academic freedom.

ANU previously declined to adopt this definition in 2023 and last year; its current position remains unclear.

Following the December 2025 extremist attack on a Jewish celebration in Bondi, intense political pressure resulted in a rushed government response – including the adoption of the IHRA definition.

This was accompanied by federal and state legislation that legal experts have assessed as unreasonably restricting the right to protest and freedom of expression.

From the unravelling of writers’ festivals to the use of force against protesters in Sydney, these measures have operated, in effect, to silence discussion of and protest against human suffering in one of the world’s most violent conflicts and severe humanitarian crisis.

Without greater political leadership and moral courage, such responses jeopardise the critical importance of addressing the rise both of antisemitism and Islamophobia, alongside all forms of racism and discrimination in Australia, including against First Nations people.

The Australian Human Rights Commission’s report on racism at Australian universities found interpersonal and structural racism “deeply embedded” in the sector.

This reinforces the urgency of identifying and responding to the drivers and enablers of all forms of racism and discrimination, consistent with human rights principles of equality and non-discrimination.

Australian universities have undergone significant change over the past three decades.

Dramatic funding cuts have shifted institutional objectives, leading to growing pressure for a reckoning.

The societal costs are visible in responses to student protests and a broader erosion of support for the physical and intellectual spaces for necessary, difficult conversations on Gaza and racism.

This has contributed to a decrease in public trust in universities, with a recent survey showing the majority of Australians expect more from our learning institutions than making a profit.

Our panel made a modest but important contribution: holding space to discuss Gaza and the wider implications of continued impunity for humanity.

The lack of consequences for sustained attacks on the UN in Gaza has emboldened adoption of the same patterns in Lebanon, including attacks on healthcare, food systems, humanitarian personnel and journalists, and dangerous disinformation campaigns that equate funding the UN with support for proscribed groups.

item.title: Learning Chinese a plus for Ryan
item.description: UNITECH student Ryan Bagah is looking forward to making a third trip to China – thanks to his efforts to learn the Chinese language on campus in Lae. “What motivated me the most was the advantages of learning Chinese, such as being able to communicate with people from different backgrounds, and having a good chance to study in China. It pushed me to keep going.”
item.content:encoded:
UNITECH student Ryan Bagah is looking forward to making a third trip to China – thanks to his efforts to learn the Chinese language on campus in Lae.

“What motivated me the most was the advantages of learning Chinese, such as being able to communicate with people from different backgrounds, and having a good chance to study in China. It pushed me to keep going.”

Ryan, 23, who recently graduated from the School of Mathematics and Computer Science at Unitech, has travelled to China twice already and is looking forward to his third trip where he will study at a university there.

His parents are from East Sepik and Western Highlands. He is the only boy in a family of four.

He completed his high school in 2021 at Fr. Peter Secondary in Banz, Jiwaka.

“I love learning foreign language during my free time. I watched YouTube videos and try practising it myself. That is, until I found out about the Confucius Institute run by a Chinese in campus. He taught the Chinese language to students.”

Ryan registered and started Chinese classes in 2024 while doing his third-year Mathematics and Computer Science course.

At first, he found learning Chinese very difficult. But over time he started to build an interest in understanding it.

Apart from speaking Chinese, Ryan can also speak a little bit of France and German both of which he learned online.

“One of the biggest challenges I faced was balancing my university studies with learning Chinese. It required a lot of discipline and time management, especially during exam time and when loaded with assignments.

“However, I stayed consistent and kept practising. It helped me improve steadily over time. I also want to prove myself that I could succeed in something outside of my main field of study.”

His first trip to China was in April last year. After returning home, he won a Chinese bridge competition on campus in May. In August, he returned to China to represent PNG in a Chinese bridge competition.

Later, he was also awarded a scholarship to study Chinese at any university in China.

 “I plan to take up the scholarship offer and return to Chongqing Normal University to study Hanyu Shuiping Kaoshi (HSK) 4 and 5 in March next year. The scholarship covers everything except air travel. So I’m saving up for that.”

HSK is the international standardized test for Chinese language proficiency that is offered at the Unitech campus. They only teach HSK 1 to 3. HSK 4 to 5 can be studied in China.

He thanked the Confucius Institute in Unitech for the great opportunity and experience that allowed him to meet so many different people from all over the world and provide him the opportunity to study overseas.

“I am proud of everything I’ve accomplished so far with the hope of achieving even more in the future.

It’s just a matter of discipline and commitment. Anyone can do anything; you just have to believe in yourself and believe in God.”

There is a saying in Chinese which, loosely translated, means: “Learning is like sailing against the current: if you don’t advance, you will retreat.” It highlights the need for continuous effort. It also emphasizes that Mandarin requires daily, active study to avoid losing progress.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: It’s time to mandate environmental spending in Indonesia
item.description: Indonesia’s flash floods in Sumatra are not merely natural disasters; they are also symptomatic of fiscal failure. While rescue teams battled torrents of mud, a quieter tragedy unfolded in the budget books.
item.content:encoded:
Indonesia’s flash floods in Sumatra are not merely natural disasters; they are also symptomatic of fiscal failure.

While rescue teams battled torrents of mud, a quieter tragedy unfolded in the budget books.

Disaster mitigation funding was systematically crowded out by competing political agendas.

It signals a concerning reality that, within Indonesia’s current fiscal hierarchy, resilience appears expendable until a disaster hits.

The crisis extends far beyond emergency response budgets.

As climate change accelerates, global norms dictate that nations should ramp up environmental spending to keep risks within acceptable limits.

These funds are critical for reforestation and upgrading ecological defences before disaster strikes.

The ministry of finance reveals a chilling fiscal paradox: 236 local governments slashed their environmental spending last year compared to the previous year, widening Indonesia’s climate finance gap, which is estimated to reach US$145 billion (about K640 billion) through 2030.

Worse still, even among districts that do allocate environmental budgets, spending bears almost no relationship to environmental quality, suggesting that the problem runs deeper than underfunding alone (see Figure 1).

The ministry of finance has attempted to steer local budgets toward environmental priorities using soft fiscal instruments.

Under Law No.1 of 2022 on Central and Regional Financial Relations, land cover indices are now factored into the formula for the general allocation fund – the main unconditional transfer from Jakarta to the regions – theoretically rewarding areas that preserve their forests.

The law also refines Revenue Sharing Funds – transfers that redistribute natural resource royalties – by compensating areas suffering from the negative externalities of resource extraction in neighbouring regions.

Furthermore, the Special Allocation Fund – a conditional grant for priority sectors – explicitly directs capital into environmental maintenance.

Yet these mechanisms remain largely incentives rather than binding mandates, leaving execution at the mercy of local political will.

The liquidity trap becomes evident when examining international climate finance as an alternative.

Most results-based instruments, such as those from the Green Climate Fund, operate on a reimbursement model where local governments must front costs and receive payments only after verified results are achieved.

Cash-constrained regions cannot sustain multi-year environmental interventions while awaiting reimbursement cycles that span several months or even years.

This funding asymmetry collides with what might be called a conservation paradox, though the reality is more complex than a simple trade-off between forests and growth.

The six Papua provinces, Indonesia’s easternmost frontier, maintain forest cover exceeding 80 per cent and harbour globally significant carbon stores.

Yet the region also records Indonesia’s highest poverty rate – around 26 per cent – despite receiving billions in special autonomy transfers and hosting the massive Grasberg copper and gold mine, one of the world’s largest.

Much of Papua’s forest cover persists not because of deliberate conservation policy, but because of remoteness and limited infrastructure.

At the same time, extraction is actively expanding: new permits for nickel mining, palm oil plantations and logging are encroaching on primary forest, with deforestation rates rising.

The paradox, then, is not that Papua has chosen conservation over development, but that neither the revenues from extraction nor the fiscal transfers from Jakarta have translated into meaningful local welfare – while the pressure to open more forest only grows.

Meanwhile, Sumatra and Kalimantan converted vast forest tracts into oil palm, timber and coal extraction zones decades ago, fuelling rapid economic growth.

Without deliberate fiscal intervention, conservation-rich regions will remain cash-starved, perpetuating a system where environmental stewardship is a burden only the poorest are expected to bear.

Given this reality, mandating environmental expenditure is not only justified but necessary.

A binding floor would force local leaders to allocate environmental budgets at a specified minimum, with failure to meet targets triggering fiscal penalties in future transfers – mirroring the existing 20 per cent education mandate.

The Supreme Audit Institution of Indonesia identified trillions of rupiah lost to governmental inefficiency in its 2024 financial report.

Beyond this, local budgets also suffer chronic bloat from inflated payroll costs that crowd out productive expenditures.

Law 1/2022 already mandates capping personnel expenses at 30 per cent of regional budgets over coming years.

item.title: Making graduation to a day of fun and laughter
item.description: HENDRICK Cassey thought that his graduation would be a quiet affair because he had told his parents not to come, convinced, that it was best that way. When Hendrick went to pick up a friend from the Jackson airport in Port Moresby before graduation day, he saw his mother stepping off the plane.
item.content:encoded:
HENDRICK Cassey thought that his graduation would be a quiet affair because he had told his parents not to come, convinced, that it was best that way.

When Hendrick went to pick up a friend from the Jackson airport in Port Moresby before graduation day, he saw his mother stepping off the plane.

“I went to the airport to pick up a friend, but to my surprise I saw Mum walking out. I laughed and said: ‘Mum, I told you not to come. But you are here.’”

He was in the second batch of graduates. After watching the first batch graduate at the Sir John Guise stadium, he went back home and was told by his guardian to have dinner as they will be leaving for the airport to pick up his Dad. Unbelievable!

“And there he was, another surprise. I told my parents not to come but they came.”

What began as an ordinary week quickly turned into one of the most unforgettable moments of his life, filled with family hugs, celebration and pride.

Hendrick, 23, whose father is from Butibam in Lae, and mother Lydia Kaivi from East Sepik, was among the 998 students who graduated from the Port Moresby Technical College recently.

He is the eldest in a family of three boys.

For him, the ceremony was more than a formal recognition of academic achievement. It was the culmination of years of hard work, late nights spent browsing over balance sheets, and most important of all, the unwavering support of his family who believed he could do it.

“It was a big challenge as a young adult trying to get through life, especially school fees and accommodation.

“My parents were very supportive from the beginning until today when I graduated. I really appreciate them.”

When his name was called, he walked across the stage with a smile that spoke volumes. In that moment, he wasn’t just a student collecting a certificate, but an individual who was a symbol of perseverance.

He was realising the dreams of many young Papua New Guineans striving to carve out a future in the world of business.

“The journey had not been easy in the past two years. I went through sleepless nights, attending classes and the routine continues. But I’m happy that I made it.”

He knows that the Diploma in Business Accounting certificate he received is going to open doors to jobs in finance, entrepreneurship and community development.

The graduation ceremony ended with laughter, hugs, photographs and embraces.

With his diploma certificate in hand, he steps into the next chapter of his life, one he knows is filled with promise, responsibility and a determination to make a difference.

Dad Hendrick Cassey Snr, was teary-eyed when asked what he felt witnessing his son graduate.

He saw it as a great achievement, one filled him with joy and pride for any parent.

“I had always encouraged my son to study hard and become someone better in the future. And now this moment is the fulfilment of that hope.”

For Hendrick, his certificate also has a deep meaning – the chance to give back, to use his skills to help local businesses thrive and to inspire others who may doubt their own ability to succeed.

He has his dreams and is fulfilling them.

In the words of one Henry David Thoreau:  “If you have built castles in the air, your work need not be lost. That is where they should be. Now put the foundations under them.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Building PNG’s future
item.description: Delivering quality education infrastructure across Papua New Guinea requires more than just construction – it demands durability, adaptability, and a deep understanding of local conditions. Through its NiuHomes division, PNG Forest Products continues to play a vital role in shaping the country’s learning environments with a comprehensive range of modular, prefabricated education facilities.
item.content:encoded:
Delivering quality education infrastructure across Papua New Guinea requires more than just construction – it demands durability, adaptability, and a deep understanding of local conditions.

Through its NiuHomes division, PNG Forest Products continues to play a vital role in shaping the country’s learning environments with a comprehensive range of modular, prefabricated education facilities.

NiuHomes manufactures an extensive portfolio of kit-set school buildings designed to meet the evolving needs of PNG’s education sector.

These include single and double-storey classrooms – such as three-in-one, four-in-one, and the newly introduced six-in-one models – alongside administration blocks, science laboratories, libraries, dormitories, ablution facilities, and staff housing, including headmaster residences.

Complementing this offering, PNG Forest Products also produces school furniture, from desks and science benches to blackboards, ensuring a complete, integrated solution.

With more than 1,000 school buildings supplied, alongside hundreds of education, health, and administrative facilities nationwide, the company has established a proven track record of delivery at scale.

Each structure is engineered to meet both PNG and Australian building codes, ensuring consistency, safety, and long-term performance.

A defining feature of the NiuHomes range is its durability. All buildings are constructed using preservative pressure-treated timber, providing full protection against termites and decay – two of the most significant challenges in PNG’s tropical environment.

This results in longer-lasting infrastructure and reduced maintenance costs for schools and institutions.

Equally important is the adaptability of the designs. The Education Range includes Contemporary, Urban, and Rural models, allowing solutions to be tailored to specific site conditions and community needs.

Prefabrication ensures efficient manufacturing and streamlined transport, enabling delivery even to remote and logistically challenging locations.

Sustainability is at the core of the PNG Forest Products operation.

All timber used in NiuHomes buildings is sourced from renewable pine plantations in Bulolo and Wau, managed in partnership with the PNG Forest Authority.

This vertically integrated approach ensures that every structure is not only built for performance, but also responsibly produced – truly “Made in PNG” from the ground up.

As Papua New Guinea continues to invest in education, reliable and scalable infrastructure solutions will remain critical.

NiuHomes stands ready to support this growth – delivering classrooms that are built to last, wherever they are needed most.
